intake.finding: The ChromeStatus entry (created 2024-08-06 by samomekarajr@microsoft.com) is a well-classified new CSS feature with a clear problem statement, named owners, a tracking bug, explainer, spec, and a sample. Microsoft is both spec author (Kevin Babbitt) and Chromium implementer (Sam Davis Omekara), with Google Chrome DevRel co-owning communication. Intake artifacts (problem, users, workaround evidence) were present from the start; the bug reference and the column-rule/multicol analogy anchor the proposal.
intake.limitations: No internal owner/decision or stakeholder map is exposed publicly; the launch_bug_url is null in the record.

incubation.finding: The developer problem is genuinely recurring and well-evidenced: CSSWG issue #2748 (long-standing request for rules in grid/flex), multiple StackOverflow questions cited in the explainer, and 'gap decorations' surfacing as a top missing feature in State of CSS 2023. The explainer states goals/non-goals (no tables, no images yet), collects use cases, considers alternatives (Mats Palmgren/Mozilla's 2021 draft; pseudo-elements) and explicitly drops logical properties based on CSSWG #10393 feedback. Incubation moved to the CSSWG venue (css-gaps-1) and the explainer was archived once adopted there.
incubation.limitations: Demand evidence is qualitative/anecdotal (issues, surveys, articles) rather than usage-based; no attributable customer/partner commitments are cited. CSSWG #10393 full thread could not be read live (two timeouts); reliance on explainer + cached-record references.

prototype.finding: A Chromium implementation exists behind the 'Enable Experimental Web Platform Features' flag (finch name CSSGapDecoration; bug 357648037 'Implement CSS Gap Decorations' with parsing CLs), an Intent to Prototype thread was filed, and the explainer/spec were iterated. However, detailed prototype-phase artifacts (task-based friction logs, explainer/spec/implementation divergence reports, compatibility matrices) were not independently available; the bug tracker is auth-gated/JS-rendered and could not be read in full.
prototype.limitations: Could not read the live Chromium bug (auth-gated) or any prototype friction log; only the existence of the implementation and intent thread is confirmed.

developerTrials.finding: A developer trial shipped in Chrome/Edge 139 behind a flag with a dedicated blog ('A new way to style gaps in CSS', 2025-06-11), an interactive playground and demos (burger menu, notebook, Daily CSS news/sudoku), an explicit call for feedback, and honest disclosure of current limitations (animating decorations; very large grid tracks). Independent community coverage followed (CSS-Tricks, 2025-07-01). The ChromeStatus record's web-developor notes document substantial post-trial enthusiasm across social media and articles, and the dev-trial goals stage explicitly sought feedback on API intuitiveness and gaps. The trial clearly informed pre-ship changes (property renames, added visibility/animation).
developerTrials.limitations: No published structured-cohort report, methodology, task-completion metrics, or participant denominator was found; feedback appears informal (social media, Chromium issues). Trial cohort representativeness across frameworks/devices/regions is not documented.

wideReview.finding: Standards engagement was broad and early: TAG review requested 2025-01-07 (#1035) with a security/privacy self-review and multi-stakeholder links; ChromeStatus records the TAG review as 'Issues addressed'. Mozilla returned a Positive position (#1158, assigned dholbert, team: Layout). The spec is a CSSWG Working Draft (First Public WD 2025-04-17). However, WebKit has given 'No response'/No signal (#444, opened same day, still open), and the ChromeStatus-tracked security_review_status and privacy_review_status both read 'Pending' at a feature that has already shipped — a documented review-gate gap (possibly stale fields).
wideReview.limitations: TAG review comments/resolution could not be read in full (issue body only); security/privacy review gates show Pending in the record; WebKit's silence is genuine (no position), not tacit approval.

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial was used: origintrial is false, no OT stage/id or trial token exists in the record, and the trial path was flag-based dev trial (M139) -> ship (M149). The TAG request body mentioned hoping to start OTs around June 2025, but the team proceeded without one. Rationale for not-relevant: this is a progressive-enhancement-only CSS visual feature with no server-side, permission, user-consent, or origin-specific setup that requires field conditions to evaluate; dev-trial-behind-flag was a sufficient evidence path. Not-relevant here means genuinely not used, not 'untested'.
experiment.limitations: If real-origin production integration evidence had been desired, an OT was the available instrument that was not exercised; the choice is defensible but undocumented as a decision.

prepareToShip.finding: An Intent to Ship thread was filed, the spec stabilized as a CSSWG Working Draft with a large WPT test inventory, MDN documentation was being added around ship (mdn/content #44435), demos/playground existed, and the stable blog documented explicit API finalization (renames: column-rule-outset/row-rule-outset -> *-inset; added *-rule-visibility-items; added animation). Progressive-enhancement fallback ('in browsers without support, gaps render normally') and a planned polyfill were communicated. The team disclosed remaining limitations.
prepareToShip.limitations: API was still churning late (property/value renames between dev trial and stable), and the ChromeStatus record was not updated to reflect ship or the resolved animation limitation (see evidenceAndCommunication).

release.finding: Release communication was coordinated and multi-channel across both implementers: 'Gap decorations: Now available in Chromium' (developer.chrome.com, 2026-05-15), 'New in Chrome 149' (2026-06-02) and Chrome 149 release notes (stable 2026-06-02), plus Microsoft's own 'New in Edge for developers' (blogs.windows.com, 2026-07-07). Coverage spanned Chrome/Edge first-party blogs with many localized editions, demos, independent press (Gigazine 2026-06-03; web-standards.dev), and community roundups. Claims matched the progressive-enhancement reality and disclosed Chromium-only availability. The single run launch event (149:Enabled by default) is corroborated by independent sources.
release.limitations: The authoritative ChromeStatus status field was not flipped to 'Enabled by default'/is_released after ship (see evidenceAndCommunication), so a release check relying only on ChromeStatus would have misfired.

adoption.finding: Chrome usage telemetry now exists as a WebFeature family counter (GapDecorations, bucket_id 437; mapping confidence 'declared-family', which is broader than this individual ChromeStatus launch and is NOT exact feature-only adoption). On that counter the feature first registered positive on 2026-05-12 at 0.000005% of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads, read 0.000208% at Chrome/Edge 149 stable (2026-06-02), reached 0.000979% at the day-30 checkpoint (2026-07-02), peaked at 0.00156213% on 2026-07-11, and was 0.0009792% on 2026-07-20 (below the peak) — very low overall, rising through the first month and then dipping. The value is the share of measured page loads on which the counter fired, not unique developers/sites/users or production deployment. No attributable production case studies with outcomes, no framework/library integration (e.g. Tailwind, design-system components), no unique-site or retention evidence, and no predeclared adoption target exist; browser availability is ~98% on the Chromium engine but cross-engine availability is zero, so real-web adoption is structurally limited. Community enthusiasm and articles exist but are not adoption. Only ~7 weeks have elapsed since stable and day-90/day-180 checkpoints are not yet available.
adoption.limitations: The family counter is not exact feature-only adoption and may cover more behavior than this ChromeStatus launch; the percentage is page-load share, not unique sites/developers/users, production deployment, task success, or end-user benefit. No unique-site, production, retention, task-success, or target evidence; day-90/day-180 checkpoints not yet available; cross-engine reach is zero so the signal reflects Chromium-only usage.

support.finding: The Chromium tracking bug (357648037) remains open and a feedback route is published, but the feature is too new and single-engine for recurring support/issue patterns, regression trends, or resolution-time data to have materialized. No public support-health dashboard or known-issues page was found.
support.limitations: Could not read live bug/issue counts (auth-gated); no post-ship support telemetry is public.

deprecation.finding: This is a brand-new additive CSS feature, not a deprecation, removal, reverse trial, or restriction. No migration, enterprise policy, or removal timeline applies; there is no replacement story because nothing is being removed. Not-relevant reflects that the deprecation/removal lifecycle does not apply.
deprecation.limitations: None; the feature is additive and progressive-enhancement-only.

developerValue.finding: The feature targets a real, long-standing, broadly felt developer problem (styling grid/flex gaps without structural hacks) with strong, independent demand evidence predating the proposal (CSSWG #2748 since 2019; State of CSS 2023 missing-feature; cited StackOverflow questions; documented workaround costs including accessibility and performance). The shipped API directly solves the stated jobs and was refined via dev-trial feedback. This success is on problem validity and fit, not inferred from shipment or sentiment.
developerValue.limitations: Demand evidence is qualitative; no proposal-specific survey with published methods.

endUserImpact.finding: End-user impact is indirect and positive in direction: cleaner visual structure and a credible accessibility benefit (the blogs argue native gap rules remove extra, a11y-tree-polluting DOM nodes and pseudo-elements used in workarounds). There is no resource, privacy, or control cost to end users. However, no end-user research or measured end-user outcomes exist, and the benefit is contingent on authors using the feature well. Net modest, unmeasured, and gated on cross-engine availability.
endUserImpact.limitations: No end-user studies; benefit is author-mediated and unmeasured.

adoption.finding: A usage signal now exists: the WebFeature family counter GapDecorations (bucket_id 437; declared-family mapping, NOT exact feature-only adoption) shows very low but initially rising page-load share — 0.000005% first positive (2026-05-12), 0.000208% at stable (2026-06-02), 0.000979% at day-30 (2026-07-02), peaking at 0.00156213% on 2026-07-11, then 0.0009792% on 2026-07-20 (below peak). This is the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired, not unique developers/sites/users, production deployment, successful tasks, satisfaction, or end-user benefit. The record carries no predeclared numeric adoption target (the dev-trial sought feedback, not a usage threshold), and no attributable production deployments, framework/library integrations, retention, or unique-site evidence was found; the feature is Chromium-only (Baseline Limited Availability). Per the method, with no predeclared target and no corroborating production/retention evidence, adoption is kept unscored and the observed level/trend is described rather than scored; low measured usage is not by itself a failure verdict and high usage would not by itself be success.
adoption.limitations: Family counter, not exact feature-only adoption; page-load share only. No unique-site, production, retention, task-success, user-benefit, or target evidence; no predeclared adoption threshold against which to score success/failure; day-90/day-180 checkpoints not yet available; cross-engine reach is zero so the signal reflects Chromium-only usage.

interoperability.finding: Standards process was strong (CSSWG Working Draft, TAG review 'Issues addressed', Mozilla Positive, extensive WPT tests, security/privacy self-review), and the team's pre-ship interop-risk assessment ('no major concerns') was reasonable. But the operative interoperability outcome at ship is single-engine: WebKit has given No signal (#444, open) and Mozilla's Positive position (#1158) is not accompanied by a shipped Firefox implementation. Consequently web-features/webstatus.dev classify the feature as Baseline Limited Availability (Chromium ~98%, other engines 0%). A positive standards position is not Baseline.
interoperability.limitations: Live WPT cross-engine pass rates could not be read (JS dashboard); only one implementation exists, so cross-engine conformance is untestable today.

implementationQuality.finding: The implementation shipped with a large, spec-aligned WPT test inventory (flex/grid/subgrid/multicol gap-decorations plus computed/valid/invalid/shorthand tests) and the team disclosed and then resolved a key limitation (animation) between dev trial and stable. Counter-signals: late API churn (outset->inset; gap-rule-paint-order->rule-overlap; a 'spanning-item' break value) shortly before ship, and disclosed residual limitations (fragmentation across paged/multicol; practical grid-size scaling limits). Single-engine, so interop-grade implementation quality is not yet demonstrable.
implementationQuality.limitations: Live WPT pass/fail and Chromium regression/bug counts could not be independently verified; residual limitations may or may not still apply at ship.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Developer communication was a clear strength (dev-trial and stable blog posts by named authors, New in Chrome 149, Edge dev blog, localized editions, interactive playground, demos, explicit progressive-enhancement guidance). However, the authoritative ChromeStatus record is materially stale after ship: as of accurate_as_of 2026-07-02 (after the 2026-06-02 stable) and again in the live chromestatuslite view it still reads Status 'In development' with is_released=false, and ongoing_constraints still lists animation as not implemented despite the stable blog (2026-05-15) confirming animation shipped. security_review_status/privacy_review_status also read 'Pending'. A retrospective or partner depending solely on ChromeStatus would wrongly conclude the feature had not shipped and lacked animation.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: The stale fields may be a record-keeping lag rather than missing work; cannot confirm whether security/privacy reviews actually completed.

support.finding: Too little time and too narrow availability (Chromium-only, ~6-7 weeks) to establish support burden, recurrence, or resolution patterns. Feedback route is published; no public support health data exists.
support.limitations: No post-ship support telemetry is public; cannot score.

overall.finding: Overall: a genuinely requested, well-incubated CSS feature that shipped on schedule (Chrome/Edge 149) via strong Microsoft-Google collaboration, with broad standards engagement, a CSSWG Working Draft, extensive tests, and high-quality multi-channel DevRel communication. A WebFeature family-counter usage signal now exists (very low but rising then dipping through ~7 weeks: 0.000208% at stable, 0.000979% at day-30, peak 0.00156213% on 2026-07-11, 0.0009792% on 2026-07-20), so usage level/trend is partially knowable at family granularity — but exact feature-only adoption, unique-site reach, production use, and retention remain not yet knowable. The binding constraints are cross-engine interoperability (Chromium-only; Baseline Limited Availability; WebKit no signal; Mozilla positive but unshipped) and a stale ChromeStatus record (status/is_released/ongoing_constraints not updated post-ship; webdx_usecounter_enum null despite a mapped family counter). Honest overall score is mixed; it must not hide the single-engine availability, the very low measured usage, or the remaining adoption/support evidence gaps.
overall.limitations: Overall conflates a successful launch-of-process with as-yet-incomplete post-launch outcomes; usage is measurable only at family granularity and adoption remains unscored. Revisit at day-90/day-180 and once usage/support detail and other-engine movement exist.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (cached record accurate_as_of 2026-07-02 + chromestatuslite.com live view)

sources.limitations: Stale post-ship: status 'In development' and is_released=false despite M149 stable on 2026-06-02; ongoing_constraints lists animation as unimplemented though the stable blog confirms it shipped; security/privacy review show Pending; webdx_usecounter_enum is null even though a WebFeature family counter (GapDecorations, bucket 437) is mapped via web-features-mappings.

sources.limitations: Editor's Draft is non-normative/work-in-progress; live cross-engine test results read from wpt.fyi separately.

sources.publisher: Chrome for Developers (Sam Davis Omekara, Patrick Brosset)

sources.limitations: First-party announcement; no participant cohort/denominator or published structured findings.

sources.publisher: Chrome for Developers (Javier Contreras, Sam Davis Omekara)

sources.limitations: First-party; pre-dates stable (2026-06-02) by ~2.5 weeks; not independent corroboration on its own.

supports: Chrome 149 stable 2026-06-02; gap decorations listed as a release highlight; progressive-enhancement framing
sources.limitations: First-party highlight summary, not a deep technical reference.

supports: Chrome 149 stable release date 2026-06-02; CSS gap decorations listed

supports: Microsoft's own 'New in Edge for developers' confirming Edge shipped gap decorations; co-implementer/partner DevRel communication; links to playground

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (WebFeature popularity timeline; cached metrics evidence collected 2026-07-22, latest data point 2026-07-20)
supports: WebFeature family counter GapDecorations (bucket_id 437; mapping status 'web-feature-family-counter', confidence 'declared-family', derived from web-features-mappings and ChromeStatus web_feature normalization — NOT a populated webdx_usecounter_enum and NOT exact feature-only adoption). 70 timeline points. Latest (2026-07-20) = 0.0009792% of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads; first positive observation 2026-05-12 at 0.000005%; at Chrome/Edge 149 stable (2026-06-02) = 0.000208%; day-30 checkpoint (2026-07-02) = 0.000979%; peak 2026-07-11 at 0.00156213%; day-90 and day-180 checkpoints not yet available.

Trend: rose from first positive (2026-05-12, pre-stable) through stable and the day-30 checkpoint, peaked 2026-07-11 at 0.00156213%, then dipped to 0.0009792% by 2026-07-20 (below peak); overall very low.
sources.limitations: Values are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired at least once — NOT unique developers, unique sites, unique users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. Scope is web-feature-family (declared-family confidence), not exact feature-only adoption: a family counter may cover more behavior than this individual ChromeStatus launch. Counter instrumentation may include detection/probing; UMA reflects participating Chrome clients and the dominant milestone, so enterprise/opt-out populations may be underrepresented; zero/missing/delayed points are not proof of zero real-world use. Timeline cached locally (sha256 bea41d91c3387d82744ff0f7dc7b166d4fc930805fc6eb2ef2596b6119fc5890); not a live re-read.

supports: Independent-style developer article during the dev trial; documents workarounds' drawbacks (a11y, markup pollution); 'only available in Chromium-based browsers'; tutorial + demo; uses pre-rename property names (column-rule-outset) confirming late API churn
sources.limitations: Author is a Microsoft Edge engineer, so partially first-party despite the independent outlet; written mid-2025 (pre-stable API).

supports: Independent press coverage: 'Google Chrome 149 stable version released, now supports CSS gap decorations' on 2026-06-03, corroborating the stable date and ship
sources.limitations: Press summary repeating the official announcement; limited independent technical depth.

supports: Independent community roundup of Chrome 149 including CSS gap decorations
sources.limitations: Aggregation/roundup; not original reporting.

supports: Gap/column-rule decorations surfaced among most-requested missing CSS features (cited in the ChromeStatus record and explainer as demand evidence)
sources.limitations: Could not re-verify the live survey page this run (relied on cached-record + explainer citations); survey has known self-selection/methodology limits and measures category interest, not API-specific demand.

successes: Genuinely developer-requested feature with multi-year demand evidence (CSSWG #2748, State of CSS 2023, StackOverflow) - the problem validity is well-supported, not inferred from shipment.

Exemplary cross-company collaboration: Microsoft authored the spec (Kevin Babbitt) and implemented in Chromium (Sam Davis Omekara) while Google Chrome DevRel co-communicated; both Chrome and Edge shipped together in 149.
Strong standards hygiene: same-day TAG/Mozilla/WebKit position requests, a security/privacy self-review, a CSSWG Working Draft, and a very large WPT test inventory.
Honest pre-ship disclosure of limitations in the dev-trial blog, with the most important one (animation) actually resolved before stable.
High-quality, multi-channel, multi-language DevRel communication with working demos and an interactive playground, framed correctly as progressive enhancement.
Progressive-enhancement-friendly design materially lowers interop/rollback risk for a Chromium-only launch.
failures: Authoritative ChromeStatus record is materially stale after ship: Status 'In development' and is_released=false as of 2026-07-02 (and in the live view) despite Chrome/Edge 149 stable on 2026-06-02; ongoing_constraints still lists animation as unimplemented though the stable blog (2026-05-15) confirms it shipped.
security_review_status and privacy_review_status read 'Pending' in the record at/after ship - an undocumented review-gate state (possibly stale, but not evidenced as complete).
Cross-engine availability is absent at ship: WebKit No signal (#444, open), Mozilla Positive but unshipped; Baseline = Limited Availability. The web feature is Chromium-only.

The ChromeStatus record's webdx_usecounter_enum field is null even though a WebFeature family counter (GapDecorations, bucket 437) is mapped via web-features-mappings and readable on the timeline; the null enum field is a record-keeping gap, and only family-granularity (not exact feature-only) usage is measurable, with no unique-site/production/retention/target instrumentation in the record.
Late API churn shortly before stable (outset->inset; gap-rule-paint-order->rule-overlap; a spanning-item break value), indicating the surface was still in flux late in the cycle.
counterfactuals: Had the team run the Origin Trial hinted in the TAG request (around June 2025) instead of the flag-based dev trial, they would have gathered real-origin integration evidence - but for a progressive-enhancement CSS visual feature with no server/permission/consent setup, the dev-trial path was plausibly sufficient and an OT likely would not have changed the outcome; the choice is defensible though undocumented.

Had Microsoft not co-driven the work (spec editor + Chromium implementer + DevRel), this would likely have remained a long-standing unmet request; Microsoft's investment was decisive to it shipping at all.

The binding constraint on Baseline/newly-available status is a second engine: a Mozilla Positive position without a Firefox implementation, plus Safari silence, means the feature stays Limited Availability regardless of Chrome execution. Earlier or stronger WebKit engagement could have changed this, though Safari's 'no signal' is not a block.

Had a retrospective or partner trusted the ChromeStatus status/is_released/ongoing_constraints fields alone, it would have wrongly concluded the feature had not shipped and lacked animation - a concrete example of why primary release signals must be cross-checked.

A WebFeature family counter (GapDecorations, bucket 437) does provide a readable usage timeline (first positive 2026-05-12, ~0.00098% of page loads by 2026-07-20, peak ~0.00156% on 2026-07-11), so adoption is not structurally unmeasurable; however, the null webdx_usecounter_enum field and family (not exact) scope mean feature-only adoption, unique-site reach, and production/retention detail are still not measurable. Had the team populated an exact feature-specific counter at ship, feature-only adoption would be directly measurable rather than family-imputed.
skillImprovements: Add a retrospective rule + regression eval: never trust ChromeStatus status/is_released/ongoing_constraints fields alone; cross-check against independent release signals (Chrome release notes, 'New in Chrome' blog, webstatus.dev/web-features Baseline) and record staleness as an evidence-quality finding.

Add a rule: when a newer primary source contradicts a ChromeStatus free-text field (e.g., stable blog says animation shipped while ongoing_constraints says not implemented), the newer source wins and the discrepancy is logged, not silently resolved.

Add outcome-dimension guidance: for cross-engine-sensitive CSS features, score interoperability on actual multi-engine availability + Baseline status, not on positive standards positions. A Mozilla 'Positive' position is not Baseline.

Add an eval: treat dev-trial enthusiasm (social media, articles) as distinct from durable adoption (exact UseCounter, attributable production case studies, framework/library integration, retention); require corroborating production/retention evidence or a predeclared target before scoring adoption, and never read a family-counter page-load percentage as unique developers/sites/users, production deployment, or success.

Add a phase check: a null webdx_usecounter_enum field does NOT imply no Chrome usage telemetry — before scoring adoption unscored, check WebFeature/WebDX family counters via web-features-mappings and the feature/timeline popularity endpoints, and record mapping scope (exact vs family vs origin-trial). A family counter is a usage signal but not exact feature-only adoption; exact, unique-site, production, and retention evidence gaps remain even when a family counter exists.

Add a not-relevant guard: when marking the experiment (Origin Trial) phase not-relevant, require explicit rationale and confirmation that no OT was actually run, so 'not-relevant' never masks an untested field path.

Add a release-phase checklist item: confirm the ChromeStatus status/is_released flip (and populate webdx_usecounter_enum / link the counter) as a closeout task alongside the 'New in Chrome' post and release notes.

missingEvidence: Exact feature-only usage and unique-site/production/retention evidence: webdx_usecounter_enum field is null and only a WebFeature family counter (GapDecorations, bucket 437) is mapped, so feature-only adoption is family-imputed rather than directly measured. No attributable production case studies, framework/library integration, retention data, or predeclared adoption target exists (feature is ~7 weeks post-ship and Chromium-only; day-90/day-180 checkpoints not yet available).
Cross-engine implementation: no Firefox or Safari implementation; Baseline Limited Availability; interoperability beyond standards positions cannot be evidenced.
Post-ship support/issue patterns and regression/bug counts: too recent and the Chromium bug is auth-gated; no public support-health data.
Live WPT pass/fail and live Chromium bug details: dashboards are JS-rendered/auth-gated and could not be read; only the spec's test inventory and bug existence were confirmed.
Full CSSWG #10393 and TAG #1035 resolution threads: timed out / only issue bodies read; relied on explainer and cached-record status references.
Published structured dev-trial cohort methodology and findings: none found; feedback evidence is informal.
